coredata.dc:description: The diversity of photoluminescence emission of multicolor carbon dots (mCDs) greatly broadens their application field. Counterfeiting is a problem that has serious consequences for individuals and society. The fluorescent ink based on mCDs has been developed due to their low toxicity and good resistance to photobleaching in the field of anti-counterfeiting. In the background, the preparation strategies of mCDs were discussed in detail, including top-down method and bottom-up method. The common carbon sources for the preparation of mCDs were further summarized, such as phenylenediamine class, amino-substituted naphthalene class, dihydroxyhenzene and benzoquinone class, biomass, and other common carbon sources. Furthermore, in order to understand the structure of CDs, this paper focused on the classification and common characterization techniques of CDs. In addition, the application of mCDs as fluorescent ink in the field of anti-counterfeiting was classified by simple anti-counterfeiting and good multiple anti-counterfeiting. Finally, the paper offered insight into the future development prospects of CDs-based fluorescent ink based on the current development trends.

head.ce:title: The development of multicolor carbon dots and their applications in the field of anti-counterfeiting

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The diversity of photoluminescence emission of multicolor carbon dots (mCDs) greatly broadens their application field. Counterfeiting is a problem that has serious consequences for individuals and society. The fluorescent ink based on mCDs has been developed due to their low toxicity and good resistance to photobleaching in the field of anti-counterfeiting. In the background, the preparation strategies of mCDs were discussed in detail, including top-down method and bottom-up method. The common carbon sources for the preparation of mCDs were further summarized, such as phenylenediamine class, amino-substituted naphthalene class, dihydroxyhenzene and benzoquinone class, biomass, and other common carbon sources. Furthermore, in order to understand the structure of CDs, this paper focused on the classification and common characterization techniques of CDs. In addition, the application of mCDs as fluorescent ink in the field of anti-counterfeiting was classified by simple anti-counterfeiting and good multiple anti-counterfeiting. Finally, the paper offered insight into the future development prospects of CDs-based fluorescent ink based on the current development trends.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are quasi-spherical carbon particles with a size of less than 10 nm [1–3], which are also considered as zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials [4–6]. They were first discovered during the preparation of single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004, and were subsequently defined in detail as nanomaterials with diameters between 2–10 nm [7,8]. The chemical composition of CDs is complex and changeable. It not only contains basic elements such as carbon, oxygen and hydrogen, but also the types and quantities of surface functional groups directly affect their physical and chemical properties [9–11]. For example, nitrogen-containing functional groups such as amino groups and imino groups can provide more electron donor or acceptor sites for CDs, thus playing a role in catalytic reactions. Due to the structural differences, CDs can be divided into graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs) and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [10,12–16]. The detailed differences between GQDs, CQDs, CNDs, and CPDs are discussed in 3.1. In addition to the inherent fluorescence characteristics, CDs also have excellent water solubility, good biocompatibility and excellent stability [17,18].

Currently, the ongoing demand for CDs is increasing in the fields of optoelectronics and biomedicine, which is attributed to the wide range of cheap precursors, easy preparation, easy surface functionalization, excellent light stability, bright PL, non-toxicity, biocompatibility and superior solubility of CDs [5,19,20]. Therefore, CDs have become a hot topic in various applications such as chemical sensing, catalysis, biomedicine and so on [21–26]. For example, Zhang employed a one-step hydrothermal synthesis method to produce dual-emitting nitrogen-doped CQDs (CECDs), and they developed a ratiometric sensor specifically designed to detect sulfite, which utilized composites formed from CECDs and Cr(VI), enhancing sensitivity and accuracy in measurement [27]. Liu et al. developed a monodisperse hollow MoC nanoreactor, where CDs were formed in situ on the surface of MoC through a carbonization reaction. They proposed that this study offers a rational strategy for preparing highly active molybdenum-based electrocatalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction by manipulating the strength of Mo-H bonds [28]. Chen et al. investigated the multifunctional applications of red-emitting CDs (rCDs) and their used in biological imaging both in vivo and in vitro through a hydrothermal method. Importantly, these rCDs demonstrated low toxicity and high biocompatibility, aligning with their excellent performance in imaging live cells, zebrafish, and nude mice [29]. However, many reported CDs mainly exhibit short wavelength regions (blue PL emission), and the single emission color can obviously limit the application of CDs in more fields [30]. Hence, the development of multicolor CDs (mCDs) is very necessary to expand their applications. For example, mCDs can find utility in the biomedical field due to their enhanced tissue penetration and reduced damage to biological tissues [31,32]. Therefore, exploring the preparation methods of mCDs and realizing their diversified applications in different disciplines play a decisive role in the development of CDs.

Counterfeiting is a problem that has serious consequences for individuals and society. Falsification of documents and the production of counterfeit currency greatly endanger the integrity of the financial system and the economy [33]. In addition, the circulation of fraudulent items has led to a loss of confidence in legitimate products and institutions. For example, counterfeits in the pharmaceutical market may cause manufacturers to bypass quality inspections and certifications, thereby undermining public health and safety [34]. Overall, the impact of counterfeit products outweighs economic losses and poses a major challenge to social well-being. In order to solve these situations, researchers have studied innovative and high-security anti-counterfeiting technologies to help people identify authenticity. The use of fluorescent ink, constructed with PL materials as their primary component, is a highly effective and practical approach to prevent counterfeiting. This method has been widely used as a valuable tool to verify the authenticity of various documents and products based on the apparent differences between exposure to ultraviolet (UV) light and sunlight. Currently, various types of PL materials, including organic dyes, quantum dots, and metal–organic frameworks, have been extensively researched for security printing applications as the fluorescent ink [34]. However, these materials often have undesirable properties. For instance, organic dyes suffer from poor light stability and a limited Stokes shift, while the preparation of quantum dots can be complicated and costly. Recently, the fluorescent ink based on CDs has been developed due to their low toxicity and good resistance to photobleaching [35–40].

In the background, this review provides an overview of the structure and properties of CDs, as shown in Fig. 1 , various methods for preparing CDs with different PL emission colors, and their applications in anti-counterfeiting. Lastly, the review offers insight into the future development prospects of CDs-based fluorescent ink based on the current development trends. This review is designed to provide in-depth insights into the practical application of CDs in anti-counterfeiting measures. Additionally, it aims to offer innovative strategies for the advancement of high-performance fluorescent inks that are centered on CDs as a pivotal component.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Classification of preparation methods for mCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation method of mCDs is the same as that of traditional CDs. In general, the preparation methods for CDs can be divided into two categories: “top-down” route and “bottom-up” route, as shown in Fig. 2 .

The top-down route involves the use of chemical or physical methods to break large-sized carbon materials into nano-sized CDs. In the top-down route for preparing CDs, many precursors were selected, such as graphene, graphene nanosheets, carbon nanotubes, and activated carbon [24,41–43]. Presently, various techniques, such as electrochemical oxidation, ball milling, ultrasonic treatment, and laser ablation, were employed in the top-down routes for the preparation of CDs [44]. Furthermore, CDs produced through the top-down routes were characterized by an abundance of graphene structures and several surface groups. Therefore, it is expected that π-π stacking may occur between the prepared CDs, which leads to a decrease in the PL intensity of CDs [24]. Although the top-down route is pioneering method for preparing CDs, it has some disadvantages, such as operational complexity, low efficiency and low quantum yield.

The bottom-up route involves the pyrolysis or carbonization of small molecules from natural or artificial sources to produce CDs, which usually contains some steps chemical reactions, including polymerization, dehydration and carbonization [45]. The bottom-up method is to prepare CDs by microwave synthesis, hydrothermal/ solvothermal method and pyrolysis method using some molecular precursors such as citric acid, sucrose and glucose as raw materials [44,46–48]. Among them, the hydrothermal/solvothermal method in the bottom-up route has become the most popular strategy in the preparation of mCDs due to their simplicity and effectiveness.

The precursors have a significant effect on the structural framework, elemental composition, degree of graphitization, and surface part of CDs, which all control the optical property of preparing CDs. In the section, some classic and extensive carbon sources for the preparation of mCDs are discussed, including phenylenediamine (PD) class, amino-substituted naphthalene class, dihydroxyhenzene and benzoquinone class, biomass, and other common carbon sources (in Table 1 ).

So far, many reports on mCDs come from benzene derivative precursors with unique structures and components, as shown in Table 1. Among them, the three isomers of PD are the most common carbon sources for the preparation of mCDs [50]. The most classical method, in 2015, Jiang et al. reported a preparation of CDs using m-PD, o-PD and p-PD as carbon sources, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3 A [51]. It was found that the prepared CDs emitted bright and stable red, green and blue colors under a single UV excitation. They believed that the change in PL emission of these CDs could be caused by the difference in their particle size and nitrogen content. Subsequently, In Fig. 3B, Ding et al, prepared CDs with tunable PL by hydrothermal method using p-PD and urea as precursors and separated by silica column chromatography [52]. These separated CDs emitted bright and steady emission in gradient colors from blue to red under single wavelength UV light. Although the particle size and graphite structure distribution of these samples were similar, the surface state of different samples was gradually changed, especially the degree of oxidation [52]. PD as a carbon source has shown great advantages in the field of preparing mCDs. Although a considerable number of preparation strategies have been developed, the quantum yield of PD-based CDs is still less than 50 % due to the formation of undesired fragments during carbonization. In 2021, in order to improve the yield of PD based CDs, Barhum et al. obtained PD based mCDs with high quantum yield through an ethylene glycol-based environment [53]. In particular, the kinetic realization of solvothermal preparation in an acidic environment improved the carbonization reaction yield of the m-PD compound and led to a quantum yield of 60 %, and the yield of the product after purification was close to 90 % [53]. In addition, PD derivatives can also be due to the preparation of mCDs. For example, Zhao et al. reported that mCDs were prepared by solvothermal method using 1,2,4,5-benzenetetramine tetrahydrochloride as carbon source and ethanol as solvent. Three different PL emission mCDs were obtained by middle chromatography isolated gel column, and the PL emission wavelengths were 619, 608 and 554 nm, respectively. It was found that there were great differences in particle size and elemental composition of three types CDs, which could have an impact on their PL emission [54]. In Fig. 3C, Zhao et al. synthesized red, green and blue (RGB) emissive CDs (RGB-CDs) by solvent heat using 3,5-diaminobenzic acid and 3,4-diaminobenzic acid [55]. The study revealed that the prepared RGB-CDs had stable optical properties and significant PL properties, independent of excitation. Structural and elemental analysis suggested that the conjugated graphite structure of the carbon core and the oxygen on the surface state were responsible for RGB emission of mCDs [55]. Furthermore, element doping is an easy and fast way to obtain mCDs based on PD derivatives. Zuo et al. proposed a fluorine doping strategy to tune the optical properties of CDs [56]. In the study, F-doped CDs were prepared by solvothermal method using 4,5-difluoro-1,2-phenylenediamine as F sources. The maximum emission wavelength (up to 600 nm, red PL emission) was much longer than that of undoped CDs, indicating that F doping produced a large emission red shift effect. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 3D, Li et al., using o-PD and 4-fluoro-1,2-phenylenediamine as carbon precursors, prepared two kinds of F-doped CDs with different F content at room temperature through different F doping routes [57]. Due to the narrowing of the band gap caused by F doping, the PL of the two F-doped CDs products achieved a significant redshift compared with the undoped CDs. Among them, CDs with high F content have a significant red shift of 70 nm [57]. In summary, the use of PD derivatives as carbon sources for the preparation of mCDs is the current mainstream, and the application of p-PD-derived CDs has also been widely developed. Meanwhile, the relevant PL mechanism is being continuously explored. We believe that in the future, the preparation of PD will not stop and enter a higher level.

The selection of amino-substituted naphthalene derivatives as carbon sources is the main method for preparing mCDs. In Fig. 4 A, Han et al. proposed the preparation of CDs with 1,4-diaminonaphthalene as the initial source [85]. The obtained mCDs exhibited strong orange emission with a maximum quantum yield of 82 % and an extremely narrow emission bandwidth of 30 nm. It was found that the orange emission of CDs was due to the high defect content including nitrogen and oxygen doping [85]. Amino-substituted naphthalene derivatives can be combined with other small molecules for the preparation of mCDs. As shown in Fig. 4B, Yuan et al. mixed citric acid with either 2,3-diaminonaphthalene or 1,5-diaminonaphthalene in an ethanol solution. Then, they conducted solvent heat treatment for different reaction times, or added an appropriate amount of concentrated sulfuric acid based on their experimental design. This led to a redshift in the RED emission and the preparation of mCDs that changed from blue emission to red emission [86].

Phenolic benzene derivatives with rich hydroxyl groups have been studied to produce mCDs. As shown in Fig. 5 A, Wang et al. used p-dihydroxybenzene, o-dihydroxybenzene and m-dihydroxybenzene as precursors by hydrothermal method [87]. Under similar preparation conditions, three series of CDs with red and yellow emission were obtained. In order to broaden the emission spectrum of CDs, the concentration ratio of hydrazine hydrate to ethanol was adjusted in the parallel reaction process [87]. Based on different adjustment schemes, new PL emission CDs could be generated, and the PL emission of CDs was precisely regulated from blue to red [87]. Moniruzzaman et al. reported a method for preparing shape-specific mCDs by controlling the reaction time and using phloroglucinol as carbon sources [101]. In the study, the six-membered ring cyclization of phloroglucinol was achieved by eliminating the adjacent active-OH and-H groups in sulfuric acid medium. In addition, it was found that wide-range multicolor PL emission of non-nitrogen-containing CDs was achieved based on solvent chromaticity. They believed that the tunable PL emission performance of the prepared mCDs could be attributed to the size of the conjugated domain and the edge electronic state containing oxygen/sulfur [101].

In 2016, Chen et al. found that in their study of the preparation of mCDs using hydroquinone as carbon sources, hydroquinone is easily oxidized in the air to transform into p-benzoquinone in the presence of oxidant, and then participate in the formation of CDs in the form of benzoquinone [88]. Hence, as an intermediate product of hydroquinone oxidation into mCDs, benzoquinone was also selected as carbon sources to directly prepare mCDs. In Fig. 5B, Liu et al. reported a one-pot method for large-scale preparation of mCDs by simply maintaining a mixture of p-benzoquinone and triethylenetetramine at room temperature [89]. Further analysis confirmed that CNDs with amorphous structure and GQDs with crystalline structure could be obtained in the CDs prepared by column separation technology, and the maximum PL emission of CNDs and GQDs is located at 518 nm and 543 nm, respectively [89].

In fact, polymers are ideal carbon sources for constructing unique structures of CDs with specific properties. At present, many polymers have been selected as preferred precursors for the preparation of mCDs. For example, polythiophene derivatives was identified as effective conjugated polymer precursors for the preparation of mCDs. Guo et al. used different polythiophene derivatives as precursors to prepare mCDs with adjustable emission range from blue to NIR at a single excitation wavelength (400 nm) [90]. The prepared CDs showed stable and bright PL in solution. They believed that the multicolor PL emission of CDs could be related to different surface states [90]. In addition to synthetic polymers, natural derived polymers such as lignin and chitosan have been used for the preparation of CDs. For example, Zhou et al. proposed a step-by-step oxidative cracking method, and then carried out organic acid engineering to achieve the preparation of lignin-based CQDs with different PL emission. Studies have confirmed that the PL, nano-morphology and surface potential of CQDs could be easily manipulated by changing organic acids. Meanwhile, by converting lignin into CQDs, an efficient waste-treatment-pollutant paradigm was constructed to achieve multifunctional water purification [91]. Zong et al. selected the biological macromolecule carboxymethyl chitosan as the carbon source and citric acid as the auxiliary carbon source [92]. CDs with multicolor PL properties and high quantum yield were prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. Subsequently, the biocompatibility of CDs with L929 cells, the antibacterial properties against Staphylococcus aureus and the efficiency of promoting wound healing in vivo were studied. The experimental results showed that the appropriate concentration of CDs had excellent biological imaging ability, which was suitable for cell growth and accelerated the healing of infected wounds [92].

It is one of the cost-effective methods to produce mCDs based on carbonization of biomass, which often require acid/alkali auxiliary conditions. Desai et al. effectively prepared water-soluble emitting CDs with specific optical properties and colors by oxidizing muskmelon fruit with acid [102]. They adjusted the PL properties of CDs by controlling the experimental conditions to emit blue, green and yellow light. Under the excitation of 342, 415 and 425 nm, the emission wavelengths of these three types of mCDs were 432, 515 and 554 nm, respectively [102]. Wang et al. synthesized four biomass mCDs by a simple one-step solvothermal method using spinach as carbon source based on the solvent control strategy [35]. The maximum emission peak of the prepared mCDs changed from blue light to yellow–red light under 365 nm excitation, covering almost the entire visible spectrum. Furthermore, the study investigated the mechanism for tunable PL based on the boiling point and polarity of the reaction solvents [35].

Citric acid is a widely used carbon precursor with many carboxyl and hydroxyl groups [103]. It is a commonly used carbon source in the production of CDs. Usually, in order to obtain CDs with different PL emission, citric acid is often used in combination with different solvents. For example, Sun et al. selected citric acid as carbon source and formamide as solvent to prepare high-efficiency red CDs (R-CDs) by one-step microwave method [95]. The optimal excitation and pure red emission wavelengths were about 540 and 640 nm, respectively. The prepared R-CDs not only have low cytotoxicity, considerable quantum yield and high photostability. R-CDs were demonstrated to efficiently bind to fluorescein isothiocyanate and transport it into living cells, which illustrated their potential as drug carriers, especially for drugs that were difficult to get directly into living cells. Additionally, due to their high photothermal conversion efficiency, R-CDs displayed excellent performance in vitro for photothermal cancer treatment [95]. In addition, dimethylformamide and dimethylsulfone could be used as solvents to dissolve citric acid, and mCDs were obtained by solvent-involved preparation strategy. Nitrogen-containing compounds, such as ethylenediamine, formamide, urea, and thiourea, could react with citric acid to prepare mCDs. For example, Miao et al. reported a method for synthesizing multicolor emitting CDs by controlling graphitization and surface function [96]. In this method, citric acid and urea were selected as precursors. By adjusting the thermal pyrolysis temperature and the ratio of reactants, the maximum emission of the resulting CDs gradually shifted from blue light to red light, covering the entire spectrum. Specifically, by controlling the degree of graphitization and the number of surface functional groups, the emission position of CDs can be adjusted from 430 nm to 630 nm. It was found that the quantum yields of blue, green and red CDs were 52.6 %, 35.1 % and 12.9 %, respectively. In addition, it was also proved that CDs could be uniformly dispersed into epoxy resin and prepared into transparent CDs/epoxy resin composites for multicolor and white light-emitting devices [96].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The structure and PL origin of CDs

ce:section.ce:para: According to the structural differences, in Fig. 6 A, CDs can be divided into four categories, including: GQDs, CQDs, CNDs and CPDs [104–106]. GQDs are nanoscale pieces of graphene that are typically smaller than 10 nm. They are characterized by having one or several graphene layers forming a carbon core with some chemical groups at the edge. The horizontal size of GQDs is usually larger than their height, and they possess unique electronic, optical, and chemical properties due to their quantum confinement and edge effects [4,16]. The structure of CQDs is characterized by spherical shapes with graphite-like lattices and functional groups present on the surface [16]. In comparison to CQDs, CNDs also exhibit spherical structures with diverse functional groups. However, a key distinction lies in the composition of the carbon core: CNDs are comprised of amorphous carbon, lacking an evident lattice structure. This sets them apart from CQDs, which possess a clearly defined lattice [10]. CPDs are aggregated or crosslinked polymer nanoparticles derived from polymers or monomers, with obvious core–shell structure. Among them, the core of CPDs has a cross-linked polymer framework with high dehydration and a small amount of graphitized carbon, and the functional groups or polymer chains located in the shell of CPDs have a great influence on their properties [10].

Soon after CDs were discovered, researchers began to investigate the PL mechanism of CDs. The study of PL mechanism is of great significance for designing CDs with tunable PL emission. Up to now, although many studies on PL mechanism of CDs have been reported, there is still some controversy about the origin of PL. CDs prepared by different components and methods usually contain different compositions and complex structures. CDs prepared by different preparation methods, precursors, and post-treatments show different optical properties, which indicates that the formation of CDs is a more complex process than expected. Therefore, it is inappropriate to establish a unified theory by comparing the properties of CDs reported in different literature. At present, there are three generally accepted PL mechanisms, including surface states, quantum confinement effect, and molecular fluorescence [107,108]. The surface state is one of the widely accepted PL mechanisms so far [109]. The surface state includes the degree of surface oxidation and the type and quantity of surface functional groups [107]. Many studies have shown that PL emission of CDs is controlled by the degree of surface oxidation. The emission redshift of CDs is closely related to the degree of oxidation on their surface. Quantum confinement effect believes that CDs, as a new zero-dimensional quantum confinement system, have unique optical properties due to the influence of the dimension and element composition of graphene [110]. Molecular fluorescence suggests that small PL molecule formation affects the PL emission of CDs [107,111].

CDs are a new type of carbon nanomaterials with unique chemical and physical properties. Several physical and chemical techniques can be used for structural analysis. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) is a microscope that images the internal structure of a sample by transmitting an electron beam. It is an important tool to characterize the morphology and structure of CDs. As shown in Fig. 6B and 6C, this technology can provide information on the particle size, shape of CDs, which is very useful for evaluating the mono-dispersity and core–shell structure of CDs. In addition, in order to further distinguish the crystalline structure of CDs, high-resolution TEM is used to observe CDs. In Fig. 6C, infrared spectroscopy (IR) is an effective method to detect the functional groups and chemical bonds of CDs by measuring the absorption spectrum of the sample in the infrared region to determine the chemical structure. It can be used to detect functional groups on the surface of CDs, such as hydroxyl, carbonyl and amino groups. In Fig. 5C, X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) is used to analyze the surface chemical composition of CDs, and the chemical state and content of elements are determined by irradiating the surface of the sample and measuring the emitted photoelectrons. The functional groups, surface impurities and oxidation states of CDs can be determined to provide important information for surface modification. In Fig. 6C, Raman spectroscopy also plays an important role in the characterization of chemical groups. It can complement with IR and XPS to jointly analyze the chemical group information on the surface of CD. In Fig. 5D, the PL spectrum is used to measure the PL properties of CDs. The intensity and spectral characteristics of the PL emission peak are determined by exciting CDs to measure the PL spectrum of their emission. In addition, the PL quantum yield and stability of CDs can be evaluated, which is the key technology to understand PL emission mechanism of CDs and optimize the PL performance. Furthermore, In Fig. 6D, the UV–visible spectrophotometer can reflect the energy absorption capacity of the carbon core structure and the surface amorphous structure on CDs by measuring their absorption spectrum in the UV–visible region, which helps to understand the optical properties of CDs, and thus qualitatively analyze their structural composition. PL lifetime is used to evaluate the optical properties of CDs and to study the mechanism of PL quenching in their detection applications. In addition to the above techniques, other techniques have been used for the structural analysis of CDs, such as atomic force microscope (AFM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), etc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of mCDs as fluorescent ink in anti-counterfeiting
ce:section.ce:para: The application of CDs-based fluorescent ink in anti-counterfeiting is mainly divided into two categories: simple anti-counterfeiting and multiple anti-counterfeiting. The simple anti-counterfeiting is to directly prepare CD into fluorescent ink for writing or printing. The obtained pattern can present the fluorescent color after the UV lamp irradiation, and then realize the simple anti-counterfeiting process. In contrast, multiple anti-counterfeiting is based on single anti-counterfeiting by adding encryption and fluorescence color conversion steps to achieve more advanced anti-counterfeiting.

The simple anti-counterfeiting of mCDs is divided into single-color anti-counterfeiting and multi-color anti-counterfeiting. Among them, single-color anti-counterfeiting, using a single mCD as the core to construct fluorescent ink, writing or printing patterns show a single fluorescence in a specific light source, which is both beautiful and achieves a preliminary anti-counterfeiting function. In contrast, multi-color anti-counterfeiting is the advanced anti-counterfeiting technology, which cleverly integrates a variety of mCD with different PL characteristics to achieve the rich and varied colors. These colorful fluorescent patterns not only have a strong visual impact, but also are difficult to copy because of their complex combination, which greatly improves the level and security of anti-counterfeiting, escorting the product, so that fake and shoddy products cannot hide.

The single-color anti-counterfeiting method is a conventional technique used to prevent counterfeiting. This method uses fluorescent ink with specific characteristics: high PL quantum yield, excellent light stability for visibility under various lighting conditions, optimal fluidity, low toxicity for safety, and quick drying for an expedited printing process. These features combined make this anti-counterfeiting method effective.

In Fig. 7 A, Tammina et al. prepared pullulan-modified nitrogen-doped mCDs by hydrothermal method [115]. It was found that the prepared mCDs had an excellent quantum yield of up to 31 %, which could be used as fluorescent inks for anti-counterfeiting applications, helping to visualize fingerprints, pictures and chemical structures under UV light, while completely invisible in sunlight and sunlight [115]. In Fig. 7B, Hong et al. studied a green method for preparing CDs from pine pollen [116]. Due to the presence of various ligands, the prepared CDs exhibited hydrophilicity and excitation-dependent emission. It was found that under the excitation of 354 nm, CDs showed the maximum emission intensity at 438 nm, which helped them to become PL materials suitable for anti-counterfeiting applications. Based on these results, they studied the application of prepared CDs in ink printing anti-counterfeiting. The printed pattern emits blue light under UV excitation, with fast drying speed, high resolution, and excellent concealment in sunlight [116].

In addition, some CDs exhibit different color emission characteristics at different excitation wavelengths, which are classified single-color anti-counterfeiting in this review. For example, Li et al. described the rapid preparation of nitrogen-doped CDs from gelatin by a sonochemical-assisted green method [117]. Compared with ultrasonic bath, ultrasound probes have higher efficiency and effectiveness in preparing CDs. Because of their good light stability and adjustable PL characteristics, the prepared CDs could be used as anti-counterfeiting inks. The water dispersion method of CDs was utilized to write two Chinese characters on cellulose paper, which were invisible under white light. However, after exposing the cellulose paper to 532 nm and 633 nm lasers, the words became bright yellow and red Chinese characters. These experiments preliminarily demonstrated the potential application of CDs in anti-counterfeiting [117].

Multi-color anti-counterfeiting technology, which integrates various PL colors based on single-color anti-counterfeiting, not only greatly improves the threshold of counterfeiting, but also attracts attention with gorgeous PL effects. The innovation is widely used in commodity anti-counterfeiting labels, which not only beautify the appearance of goods, but also significantly enhance the anti-counterfeiting performance and value of goods, becoming a new trend of anti-counterfeiting in the market.

In Fig. 7C, Zhou et al. using 2-aminoterephthalic acid and Nile blue A as reaction reagents, prepared mCDs with color tunable and high PL quantum yield by one-step solvothermal method, achieving a wide range of color field coverage [118]. Through the detailed study of the related mechanisms of blue, green and red CDs, it was shown that the regulation mechanism of multicolor PL emission was determined by the size of the sp2 conjugated domain, which was due to the increase of the particle size, resulting in the increase of the size of the sp2 conjugated domain, resulting in the narrowing of the band gap and the shift of the emission wavelength. It was found that mCDs had the advantages of simple preparation, high light stability and high quantum yield. It was used as a fluorescent ink and mixed with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to form CD/PVA composites, which was applied to the field of information encryption and anti-counterfeiting based on different PL color emission [118]. The preparation of CDs using biomass materials has attracted great attention. As a cheap and environmentally friendly material, waste leather waste can be used for the preparation of CDs and their performance adjustment. For example, in Fig. 7D, Zhang et al. selected waste leather waste as a precursor, and used only nitrogen sources (p-PD/o-PD) to regulate PL color of CDs (red, green and blue) [119]. The detailed characterization of three CDs showed that their size and functional groups could effectively increase other energy levels for electron transitions and tuning of PL properties of CDs. The PL emission wavelengths of three CDs were 612, 535 and 429 nm, respectively. In order to confirm the applicability of CDs with outstanding stability in anti-counterfeiting applications, patterns, numbers and films were developed. It has been proved that the prepared CDs had good optical properties and low cost, and had potential application value in the fluorescent anti-counterfeiting printing industry [119].

Compared with simple anti-counterfeiting, the multiple anti-counterfeiting of CDs not only improves the anti-counterfeiting performance, but also realizes the safe storage of optical information. Multiple anti-counterfeiting is divided into two categories. One is CDs themselves with special properties, which can generate a specific response signal under external stimulation, and realize the function of information encryption and decryption. The other is to combine CDs with other materials to realize the mutual conversion of PL colors under specific external stimuli, so that the anti-counterfeiting verification results are clearer, and the PL anti-counterfeiting performance is further improved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multiple anti-counterfeiting based on CDs themselves
ce:section.ce:para: The application of CDs themselves in multiple anti-counterfeiting is mainly based on the PL response signal generated under specific external stimuli and the phosphorescence characteristics. These special optical properties ensure that important information is encrypted and decrypted, which makes it difficult for forgers to replicate this special property, and the anti-counterfeiting performance is further strengthened.

The effect of environmental pH on the PL properties of CDs is often used in the field of multiple anti-counterfeiting based on CDs themselves. For example, in Fig. 8 A, Mao et al. prepared mCDs with excellent PL properties by one-step reaction of m-PD and glucose, and the PL quantum yield could reach 37 % [61]. Due to the large number of protonated amino groups and surface carboxyl functional groups, the prepared mCDs exhibited green and blue PL emission modes under acidic and alkaline conditions, respectively. Based on this situation, they produced mCDs-based inks and successfully used them for multiple anti-counterfeiting [61]. Subsequently, in Fig. 8B, Cao et al. obtained mCDs with stimuli-responsive PL chronotropic properties by a simple one-step hydrothermal method using reactive yellow 95 from waste dyeing wastewater as carbon source [120]. After adding hydrochloric acid to the mCDs solution, the PL emission peak of mCDs was red-shifted from 470 nm to 514 nm, and the PL color changed from blue to green. This phenomenon is caused by the synergistic effect of CDs surface defects and protonation. Based on acid-induced PL color rendering performance, textile anti-counterfeiting labels and dual-switch information encryption anti-counterfeiting inks were designed [120].

For the application of phosphorescence properties of CDs in the field of multiple anti-counterfeiting, Xue et al. explored a new multiple anti-counterfeiting technology through the room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) characteristics of nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) [121]. Firstly, N-CDs were successfully prepared by a simple and effective synthesis method. The obtained N-CDs exhibited excellent RTP performance and could last for 4 s, providing a feasible PL label for multiple anti-counterfeiting. Secondly, the mixing of N-CDs and sodium hydroxide could form a unique optical effect, and the PL lifetime could last for 8 ns, which improved the anti-counterfeiting ability and accuracy [121]. In order to break through the spin-forbidden nature of triplet exciton transitions, the preparation of CDs-based materials with RTP was realized. Liu et al. used the solvothermal method to prepare fluorine-nitrogen co-doped CDs (FNCDs) by gas-phase fluorination of fructose and diethylenetriamine, and their RTP lifetime and quantum yield were 1.14 s and 8.3 %, respectively [122]. By comparing the structures and properties of nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) and FNCDs, the RTP of FNCDs was determined to originate from the π-π* and n-π* electron transitions of C-N/C=N. This phenomenon was attributed to the small energy difference between the singlet and triplet states. By analyzing the hydrogen bond relationship between CDs and polyvinyl alcohol matrix, the mechanism of RTP was further discussed. The semi-ionic C-F bond also enhanced intramolecular and intermolecular hydrogen bonds, reducing the quenching of RTP without oxygen barrier. Finally, they used the prepared aqueous FNCDs as an advanced information printing and anti-counterfeiting safety ink [122].

In some applications, mCDs often need to be cleverly integrated into other materials, which mainly play the role of the substrate to provide a stable support platform for mCDs, rather than participating in the direct change of optical properties as an active element in response to external stimuli. For example, Xu et al. obtained new phosphorescent materials by embedding nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) into PVA films [123]. The RTP lifetime of N-CDs@PVA film under 420 nm excitation was significantly extended to 2.1 s, setting a new record for visible light-excited phosphorescent materials [123]. Studies indicated that hydrogen bond interaction could effectively decrease the non-radiative decay rate and radiative transition rate of triplet excitons, thus significantly extending the phosphorescence lifetime [123]. Furthermore, the practical application of N-CDs@PVA films in the most advanced anti-counterfeiting security and optical information storage fields has been confirmed [123].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multiple anti-counterfeiting based on CDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: The multiple anti-counterfeiting mechanism of CDs composite materials makes use of the unique and complex response signals of CDs and their synergy with materials, to realize the efficient and difficult to replicate the anti-counterfeiting effect [124]. This method not only enhances the complexity and security of the anti-counterfeiting logo, but also opens a new way for the field of anti-counterfeiting through the unique optical properties of CDs. Currently, there are many materials combined with mCDs, including colloidal photonic crystal (CPCs), organic molecule, fluorescent dye and microparticles.

Such colloidal ensembles are known as CPCs, which show unique light manipulation ability and structural color. It has been reported in the literature that the combination of quantum dots and CPCs achieve collective optical properties including diffracting light capabilities, photonic band gaps and fluorescence, and synergistic effects [125]. Recently, there have been reports showing that the combination of CDs and CPCs is feasible. In Fig. 8C, Guo et al. reported a strategy for the preparation of highly PL biomass-based CDs and their application in multiple anti-counterfeiting [125]. They selected food waste and turtle shells as carbon sources to develop biomass-based CDs with a quantum yield of 45 % through a carbonization process. In fact, turtle shell contains collagen, which has abundant amide, carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups. These functional groups have been proved to be beneficial to the formation of CDs. In this study, based on the high performance of the prepared CDs, the researchers systematically explored their practical application value in anti-counterfeiting and coding [125]. Firstly, through inkjet printing, CDs solution was used as ink to produce various PL patterns with simple anti-counterfeiting. Furthermore, through the combination of CDs and CPC, CPC/CD nanocomposites with distinct advantages of both unique structural color and PL color were obtained [125]. Specifically, CPC/CD beads codes with structural color and fluorescence was prepared by microfluidic technology, and CPC/CDs patterned film with fluorescence and structural color angle dependence was prepared by 3D microfluidic printing technology. They pointed out that these nanocomposites could be used for multiple anti-counterfeiting, coding and optoelectronic devices [125]. Triethyl 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylate was used as a PL center to combine with CDs forming the hybrids. In Fig. 8D, Zhang et al. used CDs and triethyl 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylate as two laser media to prepare a mixture [126]. The laser intensity ratio of the two laser bands of the mixture could be adjusted by changing the operating temperature, excitation area, and even the bending angle of the substrate [126]. Therefore, a high-security flexible multiple anti-counterfeiting technology strategy based on dual random lasing medium modulation was developed [126].

The electronic interaction between mCDs and fluorescent dyes can contribute to the preparation of multiple anti-counterfeiting fluorescent inks. Furthermore, silica is commonly used as a matrix for obtaining PL composites. Several research groups have recently achieved multi-color RTP emissions by incorporating CDs into a silica matrix. Recently, Bao et al. prepared P, N co-doped CDs with blue fluorescence and weak green RTP emission by hydrothermal method [127]. Subsequently, the obtained CDs were added to the silica matrix to develop an efficient green water-resistant RTP composite material with a quantum yield of 14.2 %. By optimizing the preparation conditions, the average lifetime of RTP was improved to 1064 ms. Furthermore, through the Förster resonance energy transfer process between CDs and fluorescent dyes, the CDs/fluorescent dye@silica composite material was constructed, and exhibited multi-color phosphorescence emission from green to red. These composites showed stable phosphorescence emission in acid, alkaline and UV light irradiation environments. Therefore, based on the unique characteristics of CDs@silica composites, a new concept of multiple anti-counterfeiting encryption and fingerprint anti-counterfeiting was proposed [127]. Subsequently, the composite material based on quantum dots and photonic crystals can produce some complex anti-counterfeiting patterns, which improves the problem of simple coding information and low anti-counterfeiting level of single-color materials. Lu et al. prepared a CDs/silicon dioxide (CDs/SiO2) fluorescent photonic crystal with angle-dependent structural color and fluorescence properties [128]. In the preparation process, 220, 250 and 280 nm silica nanospheres were prepared by changing the amount of ethanol. Then, monodisperse CDs/SiO2 composite nanospheres were synthesized by one-pot microwave hydrothermal method. After self-assembly by natural deposition, these materials have dual optical properties of structural color and enhanced fluorescence intensity, which could be used for the design and application of anti-counterfeiting patterns [128]. Fan’s group reported LiYF4: Yb3+, Ho3+, Ce3+ upconversion microparticles (UCMPs)/CDs composites were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method, which greatly simplified the preparation process of CDs and UCMPs [129]. The up-conversion wavelength could be changed by changing the doping concentration of Ce3+. Through the combination of CDs and UCMPs, an obvious dual-mode PL phenomenon was achieved. Finally, the clover pattern was made by screen printing, achieving multicolor PL emission with excitation at 365 nm and 980 nm, respectively [129].

The aim of this review is to explain the methods for preparing mCDs, such as preparation strategies and carbon source selection, as well as to discuss the main structure and characterization methods of CDs. Additionally, we deeply explore the application of mCDs as fluorescent inks in anti-counterfeiting. While there has been some understanding of the structure of CDs, there are numerous reported methods for preparing mCDs. However, a more in-depth research is required to accurately comprehend the structure and properties of CDs. The future development of multi-color carbon dots can be summarized as follows:
(1)
At present, the purity of CDs is not enough, which can affect the PL effect and stability of fluorescent ink. Although, many post-purification methods have been reported. However, solving the purity problem from the preparation stage can reduce the later purification process. In addition, the particle size distribution of CDs has an important influence on their optical properties. In order to obtain uniform PL effect and maintain the quality control stability of fluorescent ink products, it is necessary to accurately control the particle size distribution of CDs to avoid the color difference and efficiency decrease caused by uneven particle size. Therefore, in the future, it is necessary to optimize the preparation process, such as improving solvothermal method, microwave method or finding new green synthesis methods, to improve the quality of CDs.
(2)
CDs-based inks in the laboratory stage have been well applied. However, when integrating CDs-based fluorescent ink into the existing printing technology system, it is necessary to ensure its perfect integration with various ink systems to meet the needs of different printing modes. Meanwhile, the drying speed of ink should also be explored and optimized to ensure rapid curing in the high-speed printing process without affecting the printing efficiency and quality. In addition, most of the reported fluorescent inks were attached to paper, so it is another key challenge to explore and improve the adhesion of CDs-based fluorescent inks on various substrates to ensure that the patterns are bright and lasting.
(3)
With the rapid advancement of data-driven technology, machine learning, as a bright pearl in the field of artificial intelligence, is attracting unprecedented attention and exploration. Although there have been pioneering attempts to introduce machine learning into the scientific research frontier of the preparation of mCDs, the fusion application in this field is still in the growth stage, containing great potential and value to be explored. Through the efficient data processing and pattern recognition ability of machine learning, it is expected to accurately control the preparation conditions of CDs, and realize the fine optimization of color, brightness and stability, to design more complex, changeable and difficult to replicate fluorescent anti-counterfeiting labels. This exploration can promote the development of CDs as fluorescent ink, and also provide strong technical support and theoretical guidance for the innovation of anti-counterfeiting technology.

In summary, mCDs as fluorescent inks need to solve many problems and challenges in preparation technology, PL performance optimization and practical application in the future development. It is believed that mCDs based fluorescent ink can show its unique advantages and potential in more fields.
